cases.title: Derive a library from consumer use cases
cases.prompt: A CLI has parse, configure, acquire, collect, verify, detect, export, and upload stages plus a shared RuntimeContext. Design the reusable library API without preserving the CLI flowchart as its module structure. Show common and advanced consumer call sites, request/result/failure/event contracts, and the application boundary.
rubric: Begins from concrete consumers and domain language
Keeps CLI and process concerns outside the reusable core
Provides a deep facade plus independently useful capabilities
cases.rationale: The optimizer needs the central use-case-first correction.

cases.title: Prefer deep modules over shallow wrappers
cases.prompt: Review a proposed SDK with 42 public classes, each wrapping one HTTP call, plus a universal Runtime object required by every method. Explain what should remain public, what knowledge each module should hide, and how to preserve a convenient facade without forcing the runtime on all consumers.
rubric: Rejects surface-area-as-composability
Identifies change axes and hidden knowledge
Treats exports as compatibility commitments
cases.rationale: Library quality depends on depth and ownership, not class count.

cases.title: Recognize when a framework is the library
cases.prompt: A compiler toolkit lets third parties define passes, order them, inspect lifecycle events, and publish plugins. A reviewer says all Stage and Plugin abstractions are always wrong and must become plain functions. Decide whether the framework shape is justified and specify the contracts it must expose and verify.
rubric: Does not turn the guidance into a ban on frameworks
Requires evidence that extension is the product
Defines lifecycle and compatibility obligations
cases.rationale: The skill must preserve valid framework use cases.

cases.title: Map composition beyond function pipelines
cases.prompt: Design a library that uses LogTape diagnostics, receives c12-resolved configuration, stores optional checkpoints through unstorage, and offers a Hookable plugin surface. Map value, data-flow, capability, policy, ecosystem, lifecycle, package, and operational composition, with one owner per boundary.
rubric: Covers all composition scales
Preserves each ecosystem's semantics and owner
Does not equate common interfaces with common guarantees
cases.rationale: Local function composition alone misses ecosystem and operational contracts.

cases.title: Choose direct dependency or project adapter
cases.prompt: A library wants logging. One proposal imports LogTape directly with a namespaced category and leaves configuration to the host. Another wraps it as Logger.log(string). Compare the options, state when a project-owned adapter is warranted, and preserve domain events separately from diagnostics.
rubric: Rejects the lowest-common-denominator wrapper
Explains when an adapter is justified
Separates stable domain events from support diagnostics
cases.rationale: Swappability must not erase strategic ecosystem value.

cases.title: Select the smallest coherent ecosystem set
cases.prompt: An SDK already uses LogTape, c12, defu, and unstorage. A proposal adds Consola, a second config loader, a second merge library, and an internal event bus because they are all composable. Produce the ownership and exclusion decision, including where Hookable would actually be justified.
rubric: Does not maximize package count
Distinguishes companions from alternatives
Requires a real extension contract before adding hooks
cases.rationale: Composability is not ecosystem accumulation.

cases.title: Choose truthful collection and stream shapes
cases.prompt: For each operation choose and justify T, Promise<T>, readonly T[], Iterable<T>, AsyncIterable<T>, ReadableStream<Uint8Array>, or AsyncIterable<readonly T[]>: compile 500 rules, crawl 100,000 domains, decode a response body, return a final summary, and batch database writes.
rubric: Matches shape to cardinality and semantics
Uses arrays as explicit materialization boundaries
Distinguishes domain async iteration from transport streams
cases.rationale: The data shape is part of the public contract.

cases.title: Stream between subsystems and batch within them
cases.prompt: A domain pipeline yields one Observation at a time, awaits one database insert per record, logs every record, and preserves input order across skewed network requests. Redesign it with bounded admission, concurrency, buffering, ordering policy, byte/count/time batch limits, and explicit materialization points.
rubric: Separates admission, concurrency, buffering, and ordering
Batches hot subsystem work without destroying incremental flow
Accounts for slow destinations and gap buffering
cases.rationale: Per-record elegance can hide serious CPU, I/O, and memory overhead.

cases.title: Repair lazy streaming and early-return cleanup
cases.prompt: Repair collectItems() so it is a lazy AsyncIterable, reads one item at a time, stops upstream work when the consumer breaks, disposes the acquired AsyncDisposable exactly once on early return and failure, and passes the fixture verifier. Use explicit resource management where the target runtime supports it, otherwise preserve the same contract with try/finally.
rubric: Returns an async iterable rather than a materialized promise
Acquires lazily and stops after early return
Disposes on normal early return and source failure
cases.rationale: Executable proof protects incrementality and cleanup semantics.

cases.title: Design from transformations and data lifetime
cases.prompt: A technology detection library processes millions of observations. Produce a data-oriented design from target normalization through observation batches, detector index probes, fact candidates, aggregation, and persistence. Record cardinality, fields accessed, allocation, copying, lifetime, batching, and public versus internal representations.
rubric: Uses the transformation graph rather than object taxonomy
Separates stable public models from optimized internal batches
Accounts for volume, lifetime, allocation, and access patterns
cases.rationale: Data-oriented design begins with data and transforms.

cases.title: Separate hot records cold evidence and indexes
cases.prompt: Each fact candidate currently embeds a large evidence object and string technology name. The hot loop repeatedly groups and scores millions of candidates. Propose a measured internal representation, evidence store, identifiers, index lifetime, and conversion boundary while keeping the public API readable.
rubric: Reduces hot-path payload without leaking packed internals
Includes index build, memory, invalidation, and lifetime costs
Requires end-to-end evidence
cases.rationale: Hot and cold separation is useful only with explicit conversion and lifetime costs.

cases.title: Reject unmeasured typed-array redesign
cases.prompt: A 200-rule configuration library runs once at startup. A reviewer demands struct-of-arrays, numeric IDs, object pooling, and typed arrays because data-oriented design is always faster. Evaluate the proposal, define the evidence that could justify it, and choose the default representation now.
rubric: Does not equate data-oriented design with typed arrays
Chooses understandable bounded data by default
Defines conditions that would justify revisiting the representation
cases.rationale: The skill must prevent representation cargo culting.

cases.title: Model resource acquisition borrowing and disposal
cases.prompt: Design a library API that uses a shared browser pool, per-run temporary workspace, per-target browser context, database transaction, stream writer, and checkpoint session. Define owner, lifetime, borrow/transfer policy, cancellation, partial-construction cleanup, disposal order, and supported using/await using fallback.
rubric: Separates resources by lifetime
Makes ownership transfer and cleanup explicit
Handles partial failure, abort, and cleanup failure
cases.rationale: Resource lifetime is a public API concern.

cases.title: Define multidimensional workload budgets
cases.prompt: A browser-analysis library claims it is faster after raising concurrency from 8 to 64. Design a performance evaluation that covers startup, first result, throughput, p95/p99, CPU, allocation, peak and retained memory, queue depth, open browsers/pages, cleanup, and recovery under fixed admission and destination behavior.
rubric: Rejects throughput achieved through unbounded queue growth
Separates CPU, memory, latency, resource, and cleanup metrics
Defines representative baseline and correctness gates
cases.rationale: Performance is workload-specific and multidimensional.

cases.title: Prove cancellation and cleanup are separate
cases.prompt: An async iterable accepts AbortSignal and calls abort() when the consumer stops, but leased pages, a reader lock, and a temporary directory remain open until process exit. Explain the defect and design the lifecycle and tests for normal completion, early return, source error, consumer error, and cleanup failure.
rubric: Distinguishes stop requests from resource release
Covers all lifecycle exits
Preserves primary and cleanup failure evidence
cases.rationale: Abort propagation alone is not resource safety.

cases.title: Design ESM public subpaths and optional adapters
cases.prompt: Design package.json exports and source boundaries for a core analysis library plus browser, PostgreSQL, ClickHouse, LogTape, and Temporal adapters. Preserve ESM, explicit extensions, declarations, optional dependency behavior, and a small root facade without eager adapter imports.
rubric: Creates explicit stable subpaths
Keeps runtime-specific integrations in leaves
Aligns JavaScript and declaration entrypoints
cases.rationale: Selective adoption depends on physical package boundaries.

cases.title: Audit side effects before declaring tree-shakability
cases.prompt: A package sets sideEffects:false but its root imports a generated registry, configures logging, reads environment variables, and registers browser adapters. Explain why the metadata is unsafe, redesign the entrypoints, and define artifact-level tree-shaking evidence beyond bundle size.
rubric: Treats side-effect metadata as a verified contract
Moves effects behind explicit entrypoints or calls
Uses module markers or graphs plus semantic execution
cases.rationale: Metadata cannot compensate for an eagerly connected module graph.

cases.title: Repair selective package adoption
cases.prompt: Repair the fixture package so the root and core subpath expose only the core analyze capability, the browser adapter is available from an explicit browser subpath, imports perform no global mutation, package side-effect metadata is truthful, and the verifier passes. Do not remove the browser capability.
rubric: Adds explicit root, core, and browser public entrypoints
Prevents root/core imports from traversing the browser adapter
Removes import-time mutation and declares side effects truthfully
cases.rationale: Executable consumer behavior is stronger than source inspection.

cases.title: Classify restart resume and durable orchestration
cases.prompt: Classify three designs: an idempotent command reruns from the beginning, a library resumes from committed batch receipts, and a Temporal worker survives process loss with timers and signals. Define the authority and verification required for each and route durable execution ownership correctly.
rubric: Names all three guarantees precisely
Keeps library checkpoint contracts distinct from durable execution
Requires persisted authority and crash evidence
cases.rationale: Recovery claims must not collapse into one vague resumability label.

cases.title: Commit outputs before advancing checkpoints
cases.prompt: A resumable importer writes checkpoint N before flushing database batch N, and uses the current array index as its only identity. Redesign commit order, logical IDs, attempt IDs, output receipts, compatibility metadata, replay behavior, and startup reconciliation.
rubric: Prevents skipped output after crash
Separates logical work from attempts
Handles replay and incompatible resume
cases.rationale: Checkpoint position without durable output identity is fake resumability.

cases.title: Extract a reusable core without dual architecture
cases.prompt: A team wants to move every CLI stage into packages/core, preserve all runBrowserPackage names, keep RuntimeContext public, and add wrappers so no callers change. Produce a phased refactor that preserves one command, writes desired library consumers first, classifies context fields, adds a second consumer, proves selective adoption, and removes compatibility scaffolding under an explicit policy.
rubric: Does not equate package movement with extraction
Uses a vertical use-case migration
Avoids permanent dual orchestration and stale names
cases.rationale: Compatibility wrappers can preserve the architecture being removed.

cases.title: Match library claims to executable evidence
cases.prompt: Create a verification matrix for a library that claims type-safe public APIs, import safety, tree-shaking, streaming, early-return cleanup, lower memory, and checkpoint resume. Name the minimum executable evidence for each claim and distinguish source review from artifact proof.
rubric: Maps each claim to the right oracle
Includes artifact, lifecycle, performance, and crash evidence
Does not treat source inspection as runtime proof
cases.rationale: The skill must demand evidence proportional to the claim.

cases.title: Design a correctness-gated library benchmark
cases.prompt: A candidate data layout is 18% faster in one microbenchmark. Design the acceptance protocol for public parse, streaming, diagnostics, persistence, cold import, peak memory, retained memory, and error/cancellation paths. Include practical thresholds, protected workflows, raw artifacts, and rejection rules.
rubric: Keeps the microbenchmark explanatory rather than decisive
Predeclares practical and statistical decision rules
Protects memory, startup, errors, and full workflows
cases.rationale: Fast wrong or locally optimized code is not a successful library change.

cases.title: Verify a library across CLI packaging and workflows
cases.prompt: A reusable analysis library is consumed by an Optique CLI, built with unbuild, logs through LogTape, stores checkpoint metadata in unstorage, and optionally runs inside Temporal. Produce one verification plan with correct owners for CLI behavior, package artifacts, ecosystem evidence, library resume contracts, and durable workflow execution.
rubric: Uses one integrated plan without duplicate owners
Separates package evidence from public API evidence
Separates resumable library semantics from durable orchestration
cases.rationale: Library completion often spans several existing domain skills.

cases.title: Preserve cancellation and ownership across iterable-stream adapters
cases.prompt: A library adapts an AsyncIterable<Record> into a ReadableStream<Record> for fetch integration. The current adapter keeps pulling after stream cancellation, materializes pending records, and leaves the source resource open when the reader releases its lock. Redesign the boundary and explain cancellation, return(), backpressure, buffering, and ownership semantics without pretending the two protocols are identical.
rubric: Treats protocol adaptation as a semantic boundary
Stops upstream production and cleanup on cancellation
Defines buffer and ownership behavior explicitly
cases.rationale: A library can expose both protocols only if the adapter preserves cancellation, pressure, and lifetime semantics.

cases.title: Reject a runtime-selected registry that defeats selective adoption
cases.prompt: A package exposes createAdapter(name) and implements it by statically importing every database, browser, telemetry, and cloud adapter into one registry. The author argues runtime selection is maximally composable and bundlers will remove unused entries. Review the claim and design build-time selection, explicit subpaths, and an optional runtime-loading surface with truthful side-effect and dependency behavior.
rubric: Distinguishes build-time selective adoption from runtime plugin selection
Keeps the common root free of optional adapter dependency graphs
Requires artifact-level proof rather than bundler optimism
cases.rationale: Runtime registries are a common way to turn conceptual optionality into physical dependency retention.

cases.title: Route a reusable package redesign
cases.prompt: Refactor a CLI-owned TypeScript core into a public ESM library with async iterables, explicit resource ownership, optional browser adapters, and tree-shaking tests.
rubric: Activates the library skill
cases.rationale: Public reusable package work should activate the focused skill.

cases.title: Do not route an incidental private helper
cases.prompt: Rename one private formatDate helper inside an application. It has one caller and no public or reusable contract.
rubric: Avoids activating a heavyweight library workflow for an incidental helper
cases.rationale: The skill explicitly excludes incidental internal helpers.

cases.title: Keep a CLI-only flag in the CLI skill
cases.prompt: Add --quiet and generated shell completion to an existing Optique CLI. No reusable API or package boundary changes.
rubric: Routes public command language without inventing library work
cases.rationale: CLI product changes do not automatically require a reusable library redesign.

cases.title: Compose delivery CLI and library ownership
cases.prompt: Implement a refactor that keeps an Optique CLI runnable while extracting a reusable analysis library, preserving c12 precedence and LogTape routing, adding package subpaths and clean-consumer tests, and removing the old runtime API after parity.
rubric: Uses one repository discovery and integrated plan
Keeps command/config/output ownership in the CLI
Carries implementation through package proof and cleanup
cases.rationale: The most common migration requires several skills with non-overlapping owners.

cases.title: Separate library resume from durable workflow authority
cases.prompt: A library exposes resume(checkpoint), and a team claims this makes its 12-hour import durable. Design the library checkpoint contract and the Temporal worker/control-plane integration, including idempotent effects, versioning, operator controls, and crash verification.
rubric: Rejects the false durability claim
Keeps public resume semantics in the library
Assigns persisted execution and operator recovery to the workflow skill
cases.rationale: Checkpoint APIs and durable execution are adjacent but distinct.

cases.title: Research an ecosystem for a library integration
cases.prompt: Choose the smallest LogTape and UnJS package set for a reusable SDK that needs library-safe diagnostics, project config in its CLI adapter, optional checkpoint storage, and no plugin system. Inspect official siblings and exclusions, then define the library boundaries.
rubric: Maps the ecosystems before selecting packages
Keeps configuration in the CLI adapter
Avoids installing extension machinery without a requirement
cases.rationale: Library composition and ecosystem topology need separate owners.

cases.title: Deliver a selective resumable library and CLI
cases.prompt: Plan and implement a Deno-first strict TypeScript library extracted from a CLI. Preserve a thin Optique/c12/LogTape CLI adapter, expose side-effect-free ESM core and browser subpaths, use bounded async batches with deterministic cleanup, define checkpoint-resume but route durable execution separately, build and pack the artifact, verify clean consumers and tree-shaking, and remove the old public runtime/stage API.
rubric: Produces one integrated implementation and cleanup plan
Preserves all specialist ownership boundaries
Requires executable package, lifecycle, performance, and recovery evidence
cases.rationale: Frozen cross-skill topology for the full library-first migration.

cases.title: Justify a library boundary without slogans
cases.prompt: A team says library-first architecture is necessary and proposes splitting every internal module into a separately published package. Evaluate and justify the boundary decision. Name the protected objective, hard constraints, diagnosis and causal chain, credible alternatives including doing nothing or a reversible pilot, exact rejection reasons, accepted trade-offs, assumptions or defeaters, and whether the proposal is necessary, conditionally necessary, prudent, preferred, or unjustified.
rubric: Connects the situation, mechanism, constraints, and evidence to the boundary decision
Compares weaker and more reversible options before accepting package proliferation
Qualifies the conclusion and exposes evidence that would change it
cases.rationale: Library-first language must not become an unexamined package-splitting slogan.
